message.content:
You are an adversarial refuter. ASSUME this finding is FALSE and try to prove it from the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. Find the code path/contract/test that makes the PoC impossible or moot. Only survives=true if you genuinely cannot disprove it.

D: redact_creds docstring claims the primary connection string is "always scrubbed precisely"
File: src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:128
Description: redact_creds documents "the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely" via layer 1 (text.replace(conn_str, sanitized)). Layer 1 is an exact substring match against the literal conn_str. psycopg/libpq error messages do not always echo the DSN verbatim: a password can be URL-decoded, re-quoted, partially rendered, or the host/port reformatted, so the exact-match replace can miss the credential and only the generic backstops (layers 2-3) catch it — and those are explicitly described as best-effort. The word "always" promises a precision the exact-match layer cannot deliver for every backend rendering.
PoC: Construct a conn_str with a percent-encoded password (e.g. password 'a%2Fb') so libpq decodes it before embedding the decoded form in an error string. text.replace(conn_str, ...) does not match the decoded substring; layer 1 misses it and only the regex backstops (best-effort) apply — so the primary credential is not 'always scrubbed precisely'.

90	
91	# Pools opened by the factory, closed once at process exit via a single shared atexit handler
92	# (registering one handler per call would accumulate handlers across repeated factory calls).
93	_OPEN_POOLS: list[Any] = []
94	_atexit_registered = False
95	
96	# Strips ``://[REDACTED]@`` (URI userinfo) from any URI-like substring. '/' is allowed
97	# inside the userinfo so a password containing '/' is still redacted; only '@' (the userinfo
98	# terminator) and whitespace bound the match.
99	_CREDS_RE = re.compile(r"://[REDACTED]@\s]+@")
100	# Strips a libpq keyword/value DSN password token (``password=[REDACTED] / ``pgpassword=...``),
101	# handling quoted values that may contain spaces.
102	_DSN_PW_RE = re.compile(r"(?i)\b(password|pgpassword)\s*=\s*('[^']*'|\"[^\"]*\"|\S+)")
103	
104	
105	def _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str: str) -> str:
106	    """Return ``scheme://host[:port]/path`` with any ``user:password`` userinfo stripped.
107	
108	    Robust to malformed userinfo: a password containing ``/``/``#`` makes ``urlsplit``'s
109	    ``.port`` raise ``ValueError`` on access, so the whole parse (including attribute access)
110	    is guarded and falls back to the generic userinfo-strip regex / a constant placeholder.
111	    """
112	    try:
113	        u = urlsplit(conn_str)
114	        host = u.hostname or ""
115	        port = u.port
116	        scheme = u.scheme
117	        path = u.path
118	    except ValueError:
119	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str)
120	    if not host:
121	        return _CREDS_RE.sub("://", conn_str) if scheme else "<store>"
122	    if ":" in host:  # IPv6 literal — urlsplit drops the brackets; restore them
123	        host = f"[{host}]"
124	    netloc = f"{host}:{port}" if port else host
125	    return f"{scheme}://{netloc}{path}" if scheme else "<store>"
126	
127	
128	def redact_creds(text: str, conn_str: str = "") -> str:
129	    """Remove credentials a backend exception may embed in its message text.
130	
131	    Layers, in order of precision:
132	
133	    1. exact replacement of the known ``conn_str`` (handles any password characters, incl.
134	       ``/`` and spaces) — this is the precise layer and covers the real connection string;
135	    2. a generic URI-userinfo strip (``://[REDACTED]@`` → ``://``) as a backstop for any
136	       *other* URI in the text;
137	    3. a libpq DSN ``password=[REDACTED] strip for the keyword/value form.
138	
139	    Layers 2–3 are best-effort for *secondary* endpoints (e.g. a replica DSN) the caller did
140	    not pass as ``conn_str``; the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely.
141	    """
142	    if conn_str:
143	        text = text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str))
144	    text = _CREDS_RE.sub("://", text)
145	    return _DSN_PW_RE.sub(r"\1=***", text)
146	
147	
148	def get_postgres_connection_string() -> str:
149	    """Resolve the Postgres connection string (``ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL`` env → loopback default).
150	
151	    The env path bypasses any external config validation, so loopback is enforced here at the
152	    point of use (ADR-0003 defense-in-depth): graph state must not leave the box.
153	    """
154	    url = os.environ.get("ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL") or _DEFAULT_PG
155	    return require_local_uri(url, what="checkpointer connection")
156	
157	
158	def _safe_close(pool: Any) -> None:
159	    """Close one pool, swallowing errors so a single failing ``close()`` does not abort
160	    cleanup of the remaining pools at interpreter exit (``close()`` calls a C ``finish()``
161	    that can raise)."""
162	    try:
163	        pool.close()
164	    except Exception:
165	        pass
166	
167	
168	def _close_all_pools() -> None:
169	    # Snapshot under the meta-lock before iterating (#F-020): _OPEN_POOLS is appended under
170	    # _init_lock elsewhere, and on a free-threaded (GIL-less) build a concurrent list.append
171	    # reallocation during iteration is undefined behavior. _safe_close never takes _init_lock, so
172	    # closing outside the lock cannot deadlock.
173	    with _init_lock:
174	        pools = list(_OPEN_POOLS)
175	    for p in pools:
176	        _safe_close(p)
177	
178	
179	def _register_pool_cleanup(pool: Any) -> None:
180	    global _atexit_registered
181	    with _init_lock:
182	        _OPEN_POOLS.append(pool)
183	        if not _atexit_registered:
184	            atexit.register(_close_all_pools)
185	            _atexit_registered = True
186	
187	
188	def _conn_init_lock(conn_str: str) -> threading.Lock:
189	    """Return the per-conn_str bootstrap lock, creating it under the brief meta-lock (#129)."""
190	    with _init_lock:
191	        lock = _CONN_INIT_LOCKS.get(conn_str)
192	        if lock is None:
193	            lock = _CONN_INIT_LOCKS[conn_str] = threading.Lock()
194	        return lock
195	
196	
197	def _cached_pool(conn_str: str) -> Any:
198	    with _init_lock:
199	        return _POOLS_BY_CONN.get(conn_str)
200	
201	
202	def _acquire_migration_lock(conn: Any) -> None:
203	    """Acquire the cross-process schema-migration advisory lock with a bounded wait (#129).
204	
205	    ``lock_timeout`` aborts the ``pg_advisory_lock`` wait if a peer holds it past the deadline,
206	    so a stuck/crashed initializer cannot hang bootstrap indefinitely; the resulting error
207	    propagates as a redacted ``RuntimeError`` like any other backend failure. set_config is used
208	    (not ``SET``) so the value can be safely parameterized.
209	    """

content.text:
The docstring at line 140 says "the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely." The finding claims layer 1's exact substring match can miss when libpq re-renders the DSN (e.g. URL-decodes a percent-encoded password before echoing it).

Let me check whether there's any test or contract that constrains how error text is produced, to see if the "always" claim is actually defensible.

input.description: Find usages and tests of redact_creds
Search for redact usages and percent-encoding handling

content.text:
Now let me analyze the core claim. The finding's PoC: a percent-encoded password like `a%2Fb` in the conn_str. libpq URL-decodes it to `a/b` before embedding into an error message. Then `text.replace(conn_str, ...)` looks for the literal `a%2Fb` and misses the decoded `a/b`.

Let me verify the regex backstops would or wouldn't catch the decoded form, and confirm the precise-layer claim's scope.

The decoded password `a/b` would appear in a URI like `postgresql://[REDACTED]@localhost...`. Layer 2 `_CREDS_RE = ://[REDACTED]@\s]+@` matches `://[REDACTED]@` since `/` is allowed (only `@`/whitespace bound it). So layer 2 catches the URI-form. But the finding's claim is narrower: the *docstring* word "always" for *layer 1* precision.

Let me check the exact wording dependency once more and consider whether libpq actually echoes a percent-decoded DSN.

content.text:
I now have enough to evaluate the finding precisely.

The finding is a **documentation accuracy** claim, not a code-correctness claim. It targets the word "always" at line 140 for the layer-1 precision guarantee. Let me evaluate the technical substance:

**Is the PoC sound?**

1. Layer 1 is `text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str))` — a literal substring match against the exact string the caller passed.

2. The PoC's premise is that libpq/psycopg can render the credential in the error text in a form that does **not** equal the literal `conn_str`. This is genuinely true:
   - A URI like `postgresql://[REDACTED]@host/db` — libpq percent-decodes `%2Fb` → password becomes `a/b`. If the error text echoes the decoded password (e.g., in a keyword/value reformatting or a partial render), the literal substring `a%2Fb` won't be found, so layer 1's `replace` is a no-op.
   - psycopg error messages frequently are NOT the verbatim DSN — they're libpq `PQerrorMessage` strings that may render `host=... user=... port=...` keyword form, omit/reformat parts, etc.

3. So layer 1 is **not** guaranteed to match for every backend rendering — the docstring's "always scrubbed precisely" overstates layer 1's reach. In the percent-encoded case, only the best-effort backstops (layers 2–3) catch it. And the credential could still leak in renderings the backstops also miss (e.g., a bare `user=admin password=[REDACTED] is caught by layer 3, but the docstring's claim is specifically about *precise/always* layer-1 scrubbing).

**Attempts to disprove:**

- No test asserts the "always precise" guarantee. `test_redact_creds_strips_passwords_with_special_chars` only tests the case where the password appears *verbatim* in the message (`msg = f"...{conn}..."`), which trivially satisfies the literal-match layer. There is no test covering a decoded/reformatted rendering, so no contract enforces the "always" claim.
- No upstream contract forces psycopg/libpq to echo the DSN verbatim. There isn't one — libpq error strings are not specified to contain the verbatim DSN.
- The backstops do help, but the docstring explicitly labels them "best-effort," so the "always *precisely*" promise rests solely on layer 1, which the PoC defeats.

I cannot disprove the finding. The docstring's "always scrubbed precisely" is an overstatement: layer 1's exact substring match cannot match a percent-decoded or reformatted rendering of the password, and only best-effort backstops remain.

input.reasoning: Finding survives refutation. The docstring at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:140 states "the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely," attributing this to layer 1. Layer 1 is literally `text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str))` (line 143) — an exact substring match against the verbatim conn_str. The PoC is technically sound: psycopg/libpq error messages are PQerrorMessage strings not guaranteed to echo the DSN verbatim. With a percent-encoded password (e.g. `postgresql://[REDACTED]@host/db`), libpq URL-decodes `%2Fb`→`/` before use; if the error text renders the decoded form (`a/b`) or reformats into keyword/value form, the literal substring `a%2Fb` is absent and `replace` is a no-op. Layer 1 then misses entirely, leaving only layers 2-3, which the docstring itself labels "best-effort." So "always ... precisely" overstates layer 1's guarantee. Attempts to disprove failed: (1) No upstream contract forces libpq to echo the verbatim DSN. (2) The only relevant test, test_redact_creds_strips_passwords_with_special_chars in tests/test_persistence.py:35-41, embeds the conn_str verbatim into the message (`msg = f\"...{conn}...\"`), which trivially satisfies the literal match and does NOT exercise a decoded/reformatted rendering — so no test enforces the 'always precise' claim. (3) _sanitize_conn_str only affects the replacement string, not the match. The word 'always' promises precision the exact-match layer cannot deliver for every backend rendering; this is an accurate documentation-accuracy finding.

content.text:
Finding D survives refutation.

The docstring at `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:140` claims "the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely" for layer 1, but layer 1 (line 143) is a literal `text.replace(conn_str, ...)` exact substring match. The PoC is sound: a percent-encoded password (`a%2Fb`) gets URL-decoded by libpq before being embedded in error text, so the literal substring is absent and layer 1's replace is a no-op — leaving only the layers 2-3 backstops the docstring itself labels "best-effort." No test enforces the "always precise" claim (the only relevant test, `tests/test_persistence.py:35-41`, embeds conn_str verbatim, trivially satisfying the literal match), and no upstream contract forces libpq to echo the DSN verbatim. The word "always" overstates layer 1's reach.
